title: The Taíno
summary: The Taíno were the Indigenous people of the Greater Antilles — Hispaniola, Cuba, Puerto Rico, Jamaica, and the Bahamas — and the first Americans Christopher Columbus met when he reached Guanahani in October 1492. Within roughly one generation, enslavement, the encomienda labor system, massacre, and European disease shattered their society and killed the vast majority of them, especially on Hispaniola, in one of the most extreme population collapses in recorded history. For centuries they were declared extinct, but Indigenous Caribbean ancestry closely related to ancient Taíno survives in many modern Caribbean people, Taíno words and foods endure worldwide, and a modern revival movement now asserts a living Indigenous Caribbean identity.
hook: Hurricane, hammock, canoe, barbecue: the Taíno gave English all four before most of them were gone. They were the people who met Columbus on a Caribbean beach in 1492, and within a single generation enslavement and disease had killed the overwhelming majority — one of the swiftest collapses on record. Declared extinct for centuries, they were not: their descendants are overturning that colonial story now.

plainEnglish.thePeriod:
A Spanish ship reached a small island in the Caribbean Sea in October 1492. The Taíno people came down to the beach to meet it. They had lived on these islands for hundreds of years. They were the first Indigenous Americans that Columbus's expedition met. They were also the first people of the Americas written into Spain's colonial records.

The Taíno gave the world some very common words. When a big storm spins across the ocean, we still call it a hurricane. This comes from their word for a storm spirit. When you lie in a hammock, paddle a canoe, or eat food off a barbecue, you are using Taíno words too.

The Taíno lived across the large islands of the Caribbean. These included Hispaniola, home today to Haiti and the Dominican Republic. They also lived on Cuba, Puerto Rico, Jamaica, and the Bahamas. They lived in villages of houses with thatched roofs, built around an open plaza. Chiefs called caciques led them.

For a long time, people were taught that the Taíno died out completely. That is not the full truth. This is their story.
plainEnglish.theBoundaries:
The Taíno belonged to a larger family of peoples called the Arawak. The Arawak had spread north into the islands from South America. By 1492, the Taíno were the main people across the big Caribbean islands.

They were skilled farmers. Their most important crop was cassava, also called manioc. This is a root that they grated and baked into a flat bread. They also grew maize and sweet potatoes, fished the warm seas, and gathered fruit.

The Taíno were great sailors. They carved huge canoes out of single tree trunks. Some canoes were big enough to carry dozens of people between the islands. The word canoe comes from their language.

Village life had its own rhythms. People slept in hammocks woven from cotton. They played a fast ball game called batey on a flat court. They also honored spirits called zemi, which they carved into stone and wood. A cacique ruled each chiefdom, called a cacicazgo. Some caciques held power over many villages at once.
plainEnglish.theConditions:
When Columbus arrived, everything changed. It changed with terrible speed.

The Spanish wanted gold. They forced the Taíno of Hispaniola to dig for it and to hand over goods as tribute. Soon the Spanish set up a system called the encomienda. Under it, Spanish settlers were granted the labor of whole groups of Taíno. In practice, this meant forced labor, close to slavery. Many Taíno were also openly enslaved.

The work was brutal, and the punishments were cruel. People were beaten, killed, and worked to death. Families were torn apart. The food gardens that fed villages were ruined.

Then came the diseases. Europeans carried sicknesses like smallpox that the Taíno had never met before. Their bodies had no defense against them. The illness spread fast and killed huge numbers of people.

A Spanish friar named Bartolomé de las Casas saw all this with his own eyes. He wrote angry reports describing the killing and the cruelty. He begged the Spanish crown to stop it.
plainEnglish.internalDiversity:
The Taíno did not simply give up. Some fought back.

A cacique named Hatuey fled from Hispaniola to Cuba to warn the people there about the Spanish. He was captured and burned alive. Another leader, Enriquillo, led a long revolt from the mountains of Hispaniola. He held out for years before reaching a truce.

But the old Taíno world was breaking apart. On Hispaniola, the island Columbus had first settled, the fall was the steepest. At the time of contact, the island may have held hundreds of thousands of people, or even more. Scholars argue about the true number. Within about thirty years, only a few thousand were left.

This was one of the most extreme population collapses in recorded history. As the Taíno population collapsed, the Spanish brought enslaved Africans across the ocean to replace the workers they had destroyed. The Caribbean was being remade by force. The people who had named the hurricane were being pushed to the edge of survival.
plainEnglish.longConsequences:
For hundreds of years, books said the Taíno were extinct.

That story is now being corrected. The free Taíno world, with its caciques and villages, really was destroyed. Most Taíno people really did die. But not every trace was lost.

Taíno ancestry lives on in the bodies of millions of people today. Studies of DNA in Puerto Rico, Cuba, and the Dominican Republic find Taíno roots in many people there. The line of descent was never fully broken.

Their words live on too. Hurricane, hammock, canoe, barbecue, tobacco, savanna, and iguana all come from the Taíno. So do many foods and place names across the Caribbean.

And today there is a Taíno revival. People who feel a connection to this heritage gather, study the old ways, and call themselves Taíno once more. The society was shattered, and the loss was real and huge. But the people were not completely erased. The story of the Taíno did not end in 1542. It is still being written.
plainEnglish.periodizationDebate:
When does the story of the Taíno begin and end? For a long time, books framed it as a short, sad chapter. It opened with Columbus in 1492 and closed a few decades later, when the Taíno were called extinct.

That frame hides two things. The Taíno had lived on the islands for centuries before 1492. Their world was old, not new. And the supposed ending was not a real ending. People of Taíno descent are still here today.

So many people now tell the story a different way. The conquest was a terrible blow. But it was one chapter inside a much longer life. Their descendants reach into the present.

Scholars still argue about how many Taíno there once were. The true number cannot be pinned down. But almost everyone agrees on the shape of it. A huge population fell to almost nothing in about thirty years. Yet some Taíno ancestry was passed down all the same.

For hundreds of years, books said the Taíno were extinct.

The society was shattered, and the loss was real and vast. But the people were not entirely erased. The story of the Taíno did not end in 1542. It is still being written.

That frame hides two things. The Taíno had lived on the islands for centuries before 1492. Their world was old, not new. And the supposed ending was not a real ending. People of Taíno descent are still here today.

So many people now tell the story a different way. The conquest was a terrible blow, but it was one chapter inside a much longer life. Their descendants reach into the present.

Scholars still argue about how many Taíno there once were. The true number cannot be pinned down. But almost everyone agrees on the shape of it. A huge population fell to almost nothing in about thirty years, and yet some Taíno ancestry was passed down all the same.

general.thePeriod:
In October 1492, on a beach in the Bahamas the Taíno called Guanahani, a Spanish landing party met the people who would become the first Indigenous Americans in the European record. They were the Taíno, and within a single lifetime the encounter would nearly destroy them.

The Taíno were a branch of the Arawak peoples, who had migrated north out of South America into the Caribbean over many centuries. By 1492 they were the dominant population across the Greater Antilles: Hispaniola, today divided between Haiti and the Dominican Republic, along with Cuba, Puerto Rico, Jamaica, and the Bahamas.

Their importance to world history is hard to overstate, and it is double-edged. They were the first people of what later became known as the Columbian Exchange, the moment two long-separated halves of the planet collided. They were also the first to bear that collision's cost. The words they handed to the world's languages, among them hurricane, hammock, canoe, and barbecue, are now so ordinary that few who use them know they are speaking, in part, Taíno.
general.theBoundaries:
Taíno society was organized into chiefdoms known as cacicazgos, each led by a chief called a cacique, with the more powerful caciques holding sway over many villages. Settlements clustered around a central plaza, with thatched houses and a flat court for the ritual ball game called batey. Rank, ceremony, and kinship structured daily life.

The economy rested on cassava, the starchy root also called manioc, which the Taíno processed to remove its natural toxins and baked into a durable flatbread. They cultivated maize and sweet potato, fished the surrounding seas, and traveled between islands in great dugout canoes carved from single trunks, some large enough to carry many passengers. The word canoe itself descends from them.

Religious life centered on spirits called zemi, represented in carved stone, wood, and bone, and tended by caciques and ritual specialists. The Taíno slept in cotton hammocks, another of their enduring gifts to the wider world. This was a complex maritime society, neither simple nor static, with its own politics, art, and trade networks linking the islands.
general.theConditions:
Contact was catastrophic, and the catastrophe arrived fast. The Spanish came searching for gold, and on Hispaniola they subjected the Taíno to forced labor and tribute demands they could not meet. The mechanism that formalized this was the encomienda, under which colonists were granted the labor of Taíno communities. In practice it functioned as a system of coerced, often deadly, labor, alongside outright enslavement.

Massacre, starvation, and the destruction of farming systems compounded the toll. So did European diseases, above all smallpox, which struck the island in 1518 and tore through a population with no prior immunity. The biological catastrophe and the human cruelty cannot be cleanly separated; disease often killed people already weakened by forced labor and hunger.

The Dominican friar Bartolomé de las Casas, who had himself held an encomienda before renouncing it, became the most forceful eyewitness to the destruction. His writings, including his account of the devastation of the Indies, condemned the Spanish conduct in stark terms and pressed the crown for reform. His estimates of the dead were enormous, and although later scholars debate his exact numbers, the scale of the collapse he described is not in doubt.
general.internalDiversity:
Taíno responses to the invasion were not uniform, and resistance was real. The cacique Hatuey, according to the colonial record, fled Hispaniola to warn the people of Cuba, was captured, and was burned alive. Later, the cacique Enriquillo led a sustained revolt from the mountainous interior of Hispaniola, holding out for years until the Spanish negotiated a settlement. These were not the acts of a passive people awaiting their fate.

Yet the autonomous Taíno world was breaking apart under unbearable pressure. The demographic record of Hispaniola is the starkest. Contact-era population estimates vary enormously, from the low hundreds of thousands to figures well above a million, and the uncertainty is genuine, since no one counted the population before it began to fall. What is clear is the trajectory: within roughly three decades, the Taíno of Hispaniola had been reduced to a few thousand.

As the labor force collapsed, the Spanish turned to the transatlantic trade in enslaved Africans to replace it. The depopulation of the Taíno and the rise of African slavery in the Caribbean are linked chapters of the same colonial project, and the islands the Taíno had named were remade around their absence.
general.longConsequences:
For centuries, the standard verdict was simple: the Taíno had gone extinct. Reference books spoke of them in the past tense, a people who had vanished by the mid-sixteenth century. That verdict is now being revised, and the revision matters.

The autonomous Taíno world, with its caciques and its zemi worship, truly was destroyed, and most Taíno people truly did die. But the conclusion that nothing survived was a colonial one, and it was wrong. Genetic studies have changed the picture. Research on maternal DNA finds Indigenous Caribbean ancestry in modern Puerto Rico, Cuba, and the Dominican Republic. Ancient DNA from Taíno remains shows direct continuity with people living in the islands now. Descent persisted, even as the society that gave it meaning was destroyed.

The survival is cultural as well as genetic. Taíno words such as hurricane, hammock, canoe, barbecue, tobacco, and savanna entered the world's languages. Cassava bread, place-names, and other practices persist across the islands. A modern Taíno revival movement now asserts a living Indigenous Caribbean identity. The honest summary is precise. The society and most of the people were destroyed. Ancestry, words, and fragments of culture endured, and a people now reclaims them.
general.periodizationDebate:
Treating the Taíno as a closed chapter, opening in 1492 and ending with extinction around 1542, quietly adopts the conquest's own sense of where the real history lies. The colonial records are richest for the decades of destruction. That alone can make the catastrophe look like the whole story. A Taíno-centered account refuses that compression. It reads the conquest as one severe but bounded interval inside a much longer Caribbean history, with roots well before contact and descendants today.

Two questions remain genuinely open. The first is the contact-era population. Estimates range from a few hundred thousand to over a million, and the gap cannot be closed, because no one counted before the collapse. The second is how much continuity the modern revival can claim. What plainly survives is ancestry, language, and custom within mixed populations, rather than an unbroken Taíno nation. Few now dispute the near-total destruction itself, or the genetic survival. What is argued is where the story's center of gravity should sit. That choice decides whether the revival reads as a surprising postscript or as the next chapter of a continuous people.

advanced.thePeriod:
The Taíno occupy a singular position in the history of the early modern world: they are the population on which the entire demographic catastrophe of the Americas was first inscribed, and the one for which the rhetoric of total extinction was most completely accepted and is now most sharply contested. Hispaniola was the laboratory of Spanish colonialism, and the institutions that would be exported across the Americas, the encomienda above all, were field-tested on the Taíno before they were applied elsewhere. To study the Taíno is to study the originating case of a pattern that would be repeated from Mexico to the Andes.

The analytical difficulty is that the Taíno enter the written record almost entirely through Spanish eyes, and at the very moment their society was being destroyed. The chroniclers who described Taíno chiefdoms, zemi worship, and the ball game were also agents or beneficiaries of the conquest. Reconstructing the pre-contact Taíno world therefore depends on triangulating a hostile and self-interested documentary record against archaeology, linguistics, and, more recently, genetics, none of which speaks in the Taíno's own voice but each of which constrains what the chronicles can be made to say.
advanced.theBoundaries:
The category 'Taíno' is itself partly a scholarly construction, and the Advanced reader should hold it loosely. The peoples of the Greater Antilles were diverse, spoke related but distinct Arawakan languages, and did not necessarily understand themselves as a single nation; the unifying label was applied and stabilized largely by later researchers working from linguistic and material continuities. Recent scholarship has emphasized internal variation and the porousness of the boundary with neighboring groups, including the peoples the Spanish lumped under the pejorative term 'Carib.'

This matters because the contact-era sources flatten that diversity into convenient types, and because the modern revival movement must build a contemporary identity partly out of a category that was never as fixed as either the chroniclers or some present-day advocates assume. The archaeology of the Antilles reveals long sequences of migration, exchange, and cultural change reaching back well before 1200, which complicates any image of a timeless Taíno world frozen at the moment Columbus arrived. The chiefdoms he encountered were the product of centuries of development, not a primordial baseline.
advanced.theConditions:
The demographic question is where evidentiary uncertainty and moral clarity collide most violently. Contact-population estimates for Hispaniola span more than an order of magnitude, from figures around a few hundred thousand to claims of several million, and the spread reflects deep methodological disagreement about how to extrapolate from fragmentary tribute records, contested chronicler testimony, and carrying-capacity models. Las Casas's own figures have been attacked as polemical exaggeration and defended as broadly indicative; the debate over his numbers has at times been freighted with agendas about how much guilt the conquest should carry.

The analytically disciplined position holds two facts apart. The absolute pre-contact figure is genuinely unrecoverable, resting on inference rather than enumeration. The fact of near-total collapse within a generation, by contrast, is firmly established by the tribute and census records the Spanish themselves kept as the population they were taxing dwindled toward nothing. Conflating the two lets uncertainty about the starting number bleed illegitimately into doubt about the catastrophe itself, a move that has been used to minimize the destruction. The honest account refuses that slide: the magnitude of the original population is contested; the annihilation of most of it is not.
advanced.internalDiversity:
The 'extinction' thesis deserves precise dissection, because both the old claim and its correction can be overstated. The traditional position, that the Taíno ceased to exist entirely by the mid-sixteenth century, was a colonial conclusion that conveniently erased the survivors who persisted in mountain refuges, in mixed communities, and through descent into the emerging Creole populations of the Caribbean. Twenty-first-century genetic studies have made the correction concrete: research on mitochondrial DNA, which traces maternal descent, has found substantial Indigenous Caribbean ancestry in modern Puerto Rican, Cuban, and Dominican populations, and the sequencing of ancient Taíno remains has confirmed genetic continuity with living islanders.

The disciplined version of the correction is narrower than its popular form. What survives is ancestry, vocabulary, foodways, and elements of practice, carried within populations that are overwhelmingly of mixed descent, rather than an unbroken autonomous Taíno polity or community. The modern Taíno revival, which asserts a continuous Indigenous identity and has sought recognition, is a genuine cultural and political movement, but its claims about institutional continuity are contested among scholars even by those who accept the genetic and linguistic survival. Holding the line precisely, the society and most of the people were destroyed; ancestry and cultural fragments persist; and a living movement now reclaims them, which is a different and more defensible proposition than the simple denial of extinction.
advanced.longConsequences:
The deepest consequence of the Taíno is that they established the template for how Indigenous Caribbean and, more broadly, Latin American identity would be simultaneously erased and absorbed. The conquest narrative that declared them extinct allowed Caribbean societies to be reimagined as fundamentally African and European in origin, with the Indigenous substrate treated as a closed historical chapter rather than a living component. The genetic and linguistic evidence for Taíno survival therefore does more than correct a factual error; it reopens questions about the foundational ethnogenesis of Caribbean peoples that national mythologies had sealed.

The second-order consequence is methodological and continues to operate. Because the Taíno were the first case, the conceptual vocabulary used to narrate Indigenous disappearance across the Americas was calibrated on them, and the extinction verdict became a template applied wherever a colonial society found it convenient to treat the Indigenous presence as concluded. The genetic correction therefore travels: confirming Taíno survival licenses a broader skepticism toward extinction claims made about other colonized peoples on similarly thin documentary grounds, which is part of why the Taíno case carries weight disproportionate to the size of the surviving population.
advanced.periodizationDebate:
The hardest periodization problem the Taíno pose is whether bracketing them between 1492 and a putative extinction around 1542 is a description or an artifact of the conquest's own record-keeping. The colonial archive is densest exactly for the decades of destruction, and an account that weights the documentary record by its volume will reproduce the conqueror's sense that the meaningful history begins with contact and ends with collapse. The corrective, advanced by archaeologists working the pre-contact sequences and by scholars centering the modern Taíno, refuses to let 1492 mark the analytic origin: it situates the conquest as one episode within a continuous Antillean history reaching back well before 1200 and forward into the present.

The stakes are not merely framing. If the analytic center is the conquest, the genetic and cultural survival reads as an anomalous postscript; if the center is the continuous Indigenous presence, the conquest reads as catastrophic interruption and the survival as expected continuity. The evidentiary asymmetry sharpens the danger, because the thinness of the pre-contact and post-collapse record, against the density of the conquest sources, can make the catastrophe masquerade as the whole story. Privileging archaeology, linguistics, and genetics alongside the chronicles is what keeps the periodization from quietly adopting the conqueror's sense of where Taíno history begins and ends, and that contest is now actively political across the Spanish-speaking Caribbean.

foundation.theStory: The Taíno present the problem of a people declared extinct who were not, and whose destruction nonetheless was real and immense. They were the Indigenous Arawak-speaking people of the Greater Antilles, organized into chiefdoms led by caciques, who farmed cassava, sailed great canoes, slept in hammocks, and worshipped spirits called zemi. They were the first Americans Columbus met in 1492, and on Hispaniola the Spanish encomienda, enslavement, massacre, and above all introduced disease reduced a population of uncertain but large size to a few thousand within roughly thirty years. For centuries afterward they were called extinct, yet their ancestry survives in millions of Caribbean people, their words survive in the world's languages, and a modern revival movement now reclaims a living Taíno identity.
foundation.theDebate: The debates cluster around three linked questions. First, the contact-era population of Hispaniola: estimates range from a few hundred thousand to several million, and the figure cannot be fixed because no enumeration was made before the collapse began. Second, the causes of that collapse: disease, forced labor under the encomienda, massacre, and starvation all contributed, and their relative weights cannot be precisely apportioned, though the first epidemics and the labor system together carried the heaviest toll. Third, the meaning of 'extinction': whether the Taíno truly disappeared, and what it means that their ancestry, language, and culture persist while the autonomous society did not.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Taíno discipline how the story of the conquest's first victims gets told. They refuse the tidy narrative of total extinction, because their descendants and their words endure; but they equally refuse any sentimental denial of the catastrophe, because the society and most of the people were genuinely destroyed. Holding those two truths together, real annihilation and real survival, is the analytical task. The Taíno also matter because Hispaniola was where Spanish colonialism's core institutions were first built and tested before being carried across the Americas, which makes the Taíno the originating case of a continental pattern rather than a marginal footnote to it.
questions: Why can historians be confident that the Taíno population of Hispaniola collapsed within a generation even though they cannot agree on how large it was to begin with?
The Taíno were declared extinct for centuries, yet their ancestry and language survive. What does that gap reveal about how colonial societies decided who counted as still existing?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The interpretive conversation runs along several fault lines. On demography, the debate over Las Casas's catastrophic figures, and over the high-count estimates associated with scholars such as Sherburne Cook and Woodrow Borah versus more conservative reconstructions, has never fully resolved, because the underlying data are too thin to settle it. On survival, genetic research, including ancient-DNA work on Taíno remains and mitochondrial studies of living Caribbean populations, has shifted the conversation decisively away from total extinction toward documented continuity of ancestry. On identity, scholars and Taíno revival advocates contest how much institutional and cultural continuity can be claimed, with some treating the revival as a modern reconstruction and others as the recovery of a suppressed but continuous presence. The chronicle sources, principally Columbus, Las Casas, and Peter Martyr, are read increasingly against the grain, as documents of a destruction rather than transparent windows onto Taíno life.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The Taíno evidence base is sharply uneven. The pre-contact world must be reconstructed from archaeology, from Arawakan linguistics, and from Spanish chronicles produced by men implicated in the conquest, with no surviving Taíno written record to balance them. Population figures are inferred, not counted, so the scale of the original society is estimated rather than known. By contrast, the Spanish tribute and census records that track the population's decline are documentary and reliable enough to establish that the collapse occurred and was nearly total. Genetic evidence is the newest and in some ways firmest layer: ancient DNA and modern mitochondrial studies establish continuity of ancestry with a confidence the documentary record cannot match, though genetics speaks to descent, not to cultural or institutional survival. The central discipline is to keep the certain facts, the collapse and the genetic continuity, distinct from the genuinely uncertain ones, the absolute population and the degree of cultural continuity.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong account of the Taíno must do several things that pull against one another. It must state the destruction plainly and attribute it, the encomienda, enslavement, massacre, and disease, without softening the near-total collapse on Hispaniola. It must resist the false precision of a single contact-population figure, presenting the range and the reason it cannot be narrowed. It must correct the extinction narrative using the genetic and linguistic evidence, while refusing to over-swing into claiming an unbroken autonomous Taíno society survived, since what persists is ancestry, vocabulary, and cultural fragments within mixed populations. And it must present the modern revival as a real movement whose more expansive claims of continuity remain contested. The most defensible position treats the conquest as a catastrophic but bounded interval within a longer Indigenous Caribbean history, neither a clean extinction nor a seamless survival, but a destruction from which ancestry, words, and a reclaiming people endure.
questions: How should a historian use the chronicles of Columbus and Las Casas, given that they are at once the richest source on Taíno life and the testimony of the people destroying it?

If Taíno ancestry and language survive but the autonomous society did not, on what basis can the modern revival claim a continuous Indigenous identity, and what would count as evidence for or against that claim?

research.topic: The near-total collapse of the Taíno population of Hispaniola
research.content: That the Taíno population of Hispaniola collapsed to a small fraction of its contact-era size within roughly a generation is firmly established. The Spanish kept tribute and census records as they taxed the dwindling population, and these document a steep, sustained decline through the early sixteenth century to a few thousand by around 1520. The drivers, forced labor under the encomienda, enslavement, massacre, the destruction of food systems, and introduced disease, especially the smallpox epidemic of 1518, are all attested in the documentary record. What is established is the fact and trajectory of the collapse, not its precise causal apportionment or its exact magnitude.

research.topic: Genetic evidence for Taíno ancestry in modern Caribbean populations
research.content: Genetic research has substantially revised the extinction narrative. Studies of mitochondrial DNA, which traces maternal descent, have identified significant Indigenous Caribbean ancestry in modern populations of Puerto Rico, Cuba, and the Dominican Republic. The sequencing of ancient DNA from pre-contact Taíno remains has further demonstrated genetic continuity between the ancient Taíno and living Caribbean people. The current status: specialist consensus now accepts documented continuity of Taíno ancestry, while emphasizing that this establishes biological descent within overwhelmingly mixed populations, and does not by itself establish unbroken cultural or institutional continuity. The field is active and the fine-grained picture continues to develop.

research.topic: The pre-contact population of Hispaniola and the precise weight of each cause of decline
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: a systematic enumeration of the Taíno population of Hispaniola at or just before 1492, and disaggregated mortality records separating disease, forced labor, massacre, and starvation. What can be established: that a catastrophic, near-total decline occurred within about thirty years, with contact-era estimates ranging from a few hundred thousand to well over a million. What cannot be recovered: the true pre-contact figure, which rests on inference and contested extrapolation rather than counting, and the precise relative contribution of each driver, since no contemporaneous census or cause-specific mortality data exist. The fact of the collapse is certain; its exact scale and causal breakdown are not.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: A cacique of Hispaniola at contact
comparativeNarrative.name: The invasion from the side that met Columbus
comparativeNarrative.content: For a Taíno cacique in the 1490s, the strangers from the sea arrived first as a curiosity and then as a disaster. The early trading of goods gave way to demands for gold the land could not yield. Then came the seizing of people and food. From this vantage, the encomienda was not a legal term. It was the breaking of a world. The labor that had fed villages and honored the zemi was turned to Spanish mines and farms. The chiefdoms that had ordered island life for centuries fell apart within a few years. This view makes one thing clear. The catastrophe was felt as the collapse of a working society, not the fading of a primitive one.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Bartolomé de las Casas, eyewitness friar
comparativeNarrative.name: The destruction as a Spaniard came to condemn it
comparativeNarrative.content: Las Casas had held an encomienda himself before he gave it up, so his testimony is that of a participant turned accuser. To him, the dying of the Taíno was not an unlucky accident of contact. It was a moral crime for which the Spanish bore the blame, and he spent decades pressing the crown to stop it. His account of the devastation of the Indies described massacre and cruelty in stark terms. This view matters because it shows that some Spaniards condemned the destruction at the time, not only that later generations judged it. His huge casualty figures, written to move a king to act, have been read both as broadly truthful and as wild exaggeration, which is part of why the numbers are still argued over.

comparativeNarrative.name: The long view, from declared extinction to reclaiming
comparativeNarrative.content: For someone in the modern Taíno revival, the official story of extinction is the conquest's final insult. The people were killed in body and then erased on paper. From this vantage, much testifies that the line was never wholly cut. Taíno words live in everyday Caribbean speech. Cassava and other foods sit on Caribbean tables. Taíno ancestry runs in Caribbean DNA. The revival gathers people who feel that link and reclaims the name Taíno as a living identity. This view corrects the colonial belief that the Taíno belong only to the past. It also raises a careful question. What plainly survives is ancestry, language, and custom within mixed populations, and how much continuous Taíno identity that supports is part of what the movement itself is arguing about.

comparativeSummary: The Taíno look different depending on where the account begins and whose record it trusts. To a cacique who met Columbus, the conquest was the destruction of a functioning society with its own politics and religion. To Las Casas, it was a moral crime by his own countrymen, set down in figures so large they are still argued over. To a modern revival advocate, the claim of extinction was a second erasure, and surviving words, foods, and DNA now overturn it. Part of the disagreement is about evidence, over how many Taíno there were and how completely they vanished. Part of it is about framing, over whether the Taíno are a closed colonial chapter or a continuous Caribbean people. The honest picture holds the pieces together. It states the near-total destruction plainly. It presents the contested numbers without settling them. And it treats the surviving ancestry and the modern revival as real, without pretending the old society endured.

rabbitHole.label: The Columbian Exchange
rabbitHole.reason: The Taíno were the first people of the Columbian Exchange, the 1492 collision of two long-separated worlds. The disease catastrophe that devastated them, above all smallpox, was the opening instance of the biological event that would depopulate the Americas, and understanding that exchange is necessary to understand why the Taíno collapse was so swift and so total.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: Hispaniola was where Spanish colonialism's core institutions, above all the encomienda, were first built and tested on the Taíno before being carried across the Americas. The enslavement, forced labor, and dispossession that destroyed Taíno society are the originating case of the colonial pattern, making colonialism essential background to the Taíno catastrophe.

rabbitHole.label: Slavery
rabbitHole.reason: The Taíno were enslaved and worked to death under the encomienda, and as their population collapsed the Spanish brought enslaved Africans across the Atlantic to replace the destroyed labor force. The depopulation of the Taíno and the rise of Caribbean slavery are linked chapters of the same colonial project.

rabbitHole.label: The Inca Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Inca were another Indigenous American people overwhelmed by the Spanish conquest, their empire toppled and their population devastated by violence and disease. Setting the Taíno of the Caribbean beside the Inca of the Andes shows the same conquest pattern playing out at different scales and in different societies.

rabbitHole.label: The Aztec Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Aztec Empire was the great Mesoamerican civilization conquered by Spain soon after the Caribbean, and like the Taíno its people suffered catastrophic depopulation from war and epidemic disease. The comparison sharpens how the same colonial machinery and the same biological catastrophe struck very different American societies.

rabbitHole.label: Christianity
rabbitHole.reason: Missionary conversion accompanied the conquest of the Taíno, and it was a Dominican friar, Bartolomé de las Casas, who became the most forceful eyewitness condemning their destruction. The encounter with Christianity runs through both the colonization of the Taíno and the moral arguments raised against it.

rabbitHole.label: The Māori
rabbitHole.reason: The Māori are another Indigenous people colonized by a European power, devastated by introduced disease and land loss, widely expected to vanish, and instead the authors of a major modern revival. The parallel illuminates how the narrative of inevitable Indigenous disappearance has been overturned in more than one part of the world.

rabbitHole.label: The Encomienda System
rabbitHole.reason: The encomienda was the labor and tribute institution that, first imposed on the Taíno of Hispaniola, formalized the forced labor that helped destroy them. Its own dedicated entry treats the structure, spread, and long colonial afterlife of the system that the Taíno catastrophe first set in motion.

rabbitHole.label: The Moche
rabbitHole.reason: Another Indigenous American people whose world is reconstructed largely without their own writing.

rabbitHole.label: The Valladolid Debate
rabbitHole.reason: The destruction of the Taíno and other peoples prompted the reckoning at Valladolid.

reference.author: de las Casas, Bartolomé
reference.title: A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies
reference.annotation: The foundational eyewitness denunciation of the destruction of the Taíno and other Indigenous peoples, written by a friar who had held an encomienda before renouncing it. Indispensable as testimony, while its enormous casualty figures must be read as both moral witness and rhetorical instrument, which is precisely why the entry treats his numbers as contested.

reference.author: Wilson, Samuel M.
reference.title: Hispaniola: Caribbean Chiefdoms in the Age of Columbus
reference.annotation: The essential reconstruction of Taíno political organization on Hispaniola at the moment of contact, integrating the chronicle record with archaeology. The reference for the structure of the cacicazgos and the workings of Taíno chiefdoms that the conquest destroyed.

reference.author: Rouse, Irving
reference.title: The Tainos: Rise and Decline of the People Who Greeted Columbus
reference.annotation: The standard archaeological and culture-historical account of the Taíno from their Arawakan origins in South America through their settlement of the Antilles to the conquest. Foundational for the long pre-contact development that complicates any image of a timeless Taíno world frozen at 1492.

reference.author: Cook, Noble David
reference.title: Born to Die: Disease and New World Conquest, 1492–1650
reference.annotation: The key scholarly treatment of epidemic disease in the early conquest, including the Caribbean, weighing disease against labor and violence in the demographic collapse. Essential for keeping the certain fact of the collapse distinct from the contested apportionment of its causes.

reference.author: Schroeder, Hannes; et al.
reference.title: Origins and genetic legacies of the Caribbean Taino
reference.annotation: The genome-scale study of ancient Taíno remains that demonstrated genetic continuity with living Caribbean populations. The reference that anchors the entry's correction of the extinction narrative on firm evidence of surviving ancestry, while bearing only on descent rather than cultural continuity.

reference.author: Keegan, William F.; Hofman, Corinne L.
reference.title: The Caribbean Before Columbus
reference.annotation: The current synthesis of Caribbean archaeology before contact, emphasizing internal diversity and the porousness of the categories later imposed on the islands' peoples. Foundational for the Advanced argument that 'Taíno' is partly a scholarly construction over genuinely varied populations.

commerce.title: The Tainos: Rise and Decline of the People Who Greeted Columbus
commerce.author: Irving Rouse
commerce.note: If you have never read about the Taíno and want the whole arc, from their South American origins to the conquest, this is the clearest entry point, written by the scholar who organized the field. It gives you the foundation before any specialized reading.

commerce.title: Hispaniola: Caribbean Chiefdoms in the Age of Columbus
commerce.author: Samuel M. Wilson
commerce.note: Read this when you want to understand how Taíno society actually worked on the eve of contact, with its caciques and chiefdoms reconstructed from both the chronicles and the archaeology. The book to reach for to see what the conquest destroyed.

commerce.title: Born to Die: Disease and New World Conquest, 1492–1650
commerce.author: Noble David Cook
commerce.note: Turn to this to understand the disease catastrophe that struck the Taíno and the wider Americas, and how historians weigh disease against violence and forced labor. The place to grasp why the collapse was so swift without losing the human responsibility for it.

commerce.title: A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies
commerce.author: Bartolomé de las Casas
commerce.note: Read the original eyewitness denunciation when you are ready to confront the conquest in the words of a Spaniard who condemned it in 1552. Demanding and disturbing, and best read knowing his huge casualty figures are both moral testimony and rhetoric.

commerce.title: The Caribbean Before Columbus
commerce.author: William F. Keegan and Corinne L. Hofman
commerce.note: For readers ready for the major archaeological synthesis, this reconstructs the deep Indigenous history of the islands and shows how varied the peoples lumped together as Taíno really were. Field-defining and best after a general grounding in the conquest.

popularCulture.title: The words hurricane, hammock, and canoe
popularCulture.description: Some of the most common words in English came from the Taíno. Hurricane, hammock, canoe, barbecue, tobacco, and savanna all trace back to them. Most people use these words with no idea where they came from. The words are a quiet proof that the Taíno shaped the world long after they were said to be gone. But they also hide the people behind them, who are rarely named.

popularCulture.title: The myth that the Taíno went extinct
popularCulture.description: For a long time, schoolbooks taught that the Taíno died out completely. That idea is still very common. The truth is more complicated. The old Taíno world and most of its people really were destroyed. But Taíno ancestry lives on in millions of Caribbean people today, and their words and foods survive. The neat story of total extinction was a colonial idea, not a fact.

popularCulture.title: Cassava and Caribbean food
popularCulture.description: Cassava bread, the flat bread the Taíno baked from a grated root, is still eaten across the Caribbean. So are foods and cooking ways that reach back to them. People enjoy this food as part of island life without knowing its Taíno roots. It is a living link to the first people of the islands. Yet the recipes have also changed over five centuries and mixed with African and Spanish cooking.

popularCulture.title: The modern Taíno revival
popularCulture.description: Today some people across the Caribbean and its diaspora call themselves Taíno again. They gather, study the old ways, and reclaim the name their ancestors carried. This movement says plainly that the Taíno never fully vanished. It is real and growing. But it also raises hard questions, because what clearly survives is ancestry, words, and customs within mixed communities, not an unbroken Taíno nation.

popularCulture.title: Hatuey, the burned cacique
popularCulture.description: The cacique Hatuey, burned alive by the Spanish for resisting, became a hero of memory in Cuba. His name appears on a beer, a town, and in patriotic stories. He stands for the courage to fight the conquest. The retellings can flatten him into a simple symbol. The colonial account of his death is also the only one we have, told by the very people who killed him.
